title: Splice: Remove at Index
description: Given an array and an index, remove exactly one element at that index using `.splice()` and return the modified array. Note that `.splice()` mutates the original array in place.
whatShouldHappen: Call .splice() on the array, passing the index as the first argument.
Pass 1 as the second argument to specify that one element should be deleted.
Return the modified array.
patternExplanation: Splice takes a start index and a delete count. It mutates the array in place and returns an array of the deleted items.

title: Splice: Remove a Chunk
description: Given an array, a start index, and a count `k`, remove `k` elements starting from that index and return the modified array.
whatShouldHappen: Use .splice(startIndex, k) to remove the specified number of elements.
Return the mutated array.
patternExplanation: The second argument to splice determines how many elements are removed. If it exceeds the remaining length, it removes all elements to the end.

title: Splice: Replace Element
description: Given an array, an index, and a new item, replace the element at that index with the new item using `.splice()`. Return the modified array.
whatShouldHappen: Call .splice() starting at the given index.
Set the delete count to 1 (to remove the old item).
Provide the new item as the third argument to insert it in place of the deleted one.
Return the array.
patternExplanation: By setting deleteCount to 1 and providing new items, splice acts as a replacement tool.

title: Splice: Remove Last N Elements
description: Given an array and a number `n`, remove the last `n` elements from the array using `.splice()` and a negative index. Return the modified array. Assume `n` is greater than 0 and less than or equal to the array length.
whatShouldHappen: Call .splice() using -n as the start index.
This tells splice to start counting from the end of the array.
You don't strictly need a delete count if you want to remove everything from that point to the end.
Return the modified array.
patternExplanation: Negative indices in slice and splice count backwards from the end of the array. -1 is the last element.

title: Splice: Insert Multiple
description: Given an array, an index, and an array of new items, insert all the new items into the array at the given index without removing anything. Use the spread operator `...` with `.splice()`. Return the array.
whatShouldHappen: Call .splice() with the given index and 0 for the delete count.
Use the spread operator (...newItems) to pass the elements of the new items array as separate arguments to splice.
Return the modified array.
patternExplanation: Since splice takes individual arguments for items to insert, spreading an array into it is the perfect way to insert a whole array into another.

title: Splice: Swap Right
description: Given an array and an index, swap the element at that index with the element immediately to its right using exactly one call to `.splice()`. If the index is the last element, do nothing. Return the modified array.
whatShouldHappen: Check if the index is at the end of the array. If so, return early.
Use splice to remove 2 elements starting at the given index.
Since splice returns the deleted elements, you can save them in an array.
In the same splice call, insert them back in reverse order, OR do it in two steps: splice to extract, then splice to insert.
Actually, there's a trick: `arr.splice(index, 2, arr[index+1], arr[index])`!
patternExplanation: Splice can simultaneously remove and insert, making it possible to reorder elements in a single operation without temporary variables.

title: Splice: Extract Middle
description: Given an array, extract and return the middle element. If the array length is even, extract and return the middle *two* elements. Mutate the original array using `.splice()`.
whatShouldHappen: Calculate the middle index.
Determine if the length is odd or even.
If odd, splice out 1 element at the middle index.
If even, splice out 2 elements starting at (middle - 1).
Return the array of removed elements (which is what .splice() returns natively).
patternExplanation: Splice returns an array containing the deleted elements, making it ideal for extracting data destructively.

title: Splice: Clear Array
description: Empty an array completely using `.splice()` and return the now-empty array. (Do not just reassign the variable to `[]`, you must mutate the original reference).
whatShouldHappen: Call .splice() starting at index 0.
Do not provide a second argument, which tells splice to delete everything until the end.
Return the array.
patternExplanation: If deleteCount is omitted, splice removes all elements from start to the end of the array.

title: Splice: Remove Evens Safely
description: Given an array of numbers, remove all even numbers *in-place* using `.splice()`. To avoid index shifting bugs, iterate through the array backwards. Return the modified array.
whatShouldHappen: Set up a for loop that starts at `arr.length - 1` and counts down to 0.
If the current element is even, splice it out.
Iterating backwards ensures that when you remove an element, it only shifts the indices of elements you have *already processed*, keeping the ones you haven't processed at their correct indices.
patternExplanation: When removing elements in a loop, iterating forwards causes elements to skip indices as the array shrinks. Iterating backwards perfectly avoids this.

title: Splice: Move Element
description: Given an array, a `fromIndex`, and a `toIndex`, move the element at `fromIndex` to `toIndex` in-place using `.splice()`. Return the modified array. Assume both indices are valid and within bounds.
whatShouldHappen: Use splice to remove 1 element at the `fromIndex`.
Save the extracted element (remember splice returns an array).
Use splice again to insert that extracted element at the `toIndex`.
Return the array.
patternExplanation: Splice can extract elements into a temporary variable, which can then be re-inserted elsewhere, making it useful for drag-and-drop or reordering logic.
